concerns.text: The plan targets 30m resolution for the entire city grid (~100M pixels) but fits Geographically Weighted Regression (GWR) and SAR models. GWR is computationally O(N^2) or O(N^3) depending on implementation and is intractable for N > 100k on CPU. Fitting GWR on millions of pixels will fail the 6-hour runtime and 7GB RAM constraints, rendering the core analysis impossible as designed.

The plan proposes fitting GWR on a 30m grid (millions of observations). Standard GWR implementations (e.g., mgwr) do not scale to this N without massive subsampling or approximation. The plan lacks a strategy (e.g., block-wise fitting, subsampling, or using a different estimator) to make this statistically valid and computationally feasible, risking a null result due to resource exhaustion.

The permutation test described ('permuting OSM features to falsify the null') ignores spatial autocorrelation. Permuting raw values destroys the spatial structure of the predictors, creating a null distribution that is not comparable to the observed spatially autocorrelated data. A valid spatial permutation test (e.g., toroidal shift or restricted permutation) is required to maintain the spatial dependence structure under the null hypothesis.

The plan uses MODIS (1km native) and Landsat (30m) data simultaneously. While Landsat is 30m, MODIS LST products (MOD11A1) are typically 1km. Upscaling MODIS to 30m via interpolation creates artificial precision and does not add information, potentially introducing noise or artifacts. The plan must clarify if MODIS is used only for temporal compositing (downscaled) or if the analysis relies primarily on Landsat, as mixing resolutions without rigorous downscaling methodology is scientifically unsound.
